name: Taylor — Canadian Gen Z
description: A 22-year-old from Toronto who discovers new beverages through TikTok and Instagram. Swapped soda for sparkling water two years ago and cares about Canadian brands.
pain_points: hard to find in convenience stores
unfamiliar with the brand's current lineup
price higher than energy drinks
buying_triggers: sees it go viral on TikTok
behavior_rules: Will not buy if she can't find it within walking distance of home or work
Always checks Instagram to see if her friends have posted about a brand before buying
Skips any beverage with more than 5g of sugar
Will repost an aesthetically packaged product unprompted

name: Marcus — Canadian Millennial
description: A 34-year-old in Vancouver who grew up drinking Clearly Canadian. Switched to sparkling water three years ago for health reasons and would recognize the brand immediately.
pain_points: sugar content in Originals feels high
hard to find at his grocery store
not sure if product is the same as he remembers
buying_triggers: sees it on a grocery shelf display
objections: 'The original had a lot of sugar'
'Is this the same as the 90s version?'
'Why would I pay more than San Pellegrino?'
behavior_rules: Reads the nutrition label on any sparkling water before adding to cart
Won't pay more than $3 for a single-serve if he doesn't know the brand
Always tries a known brand before switching to something new
Buys in bulk when he finds a sparkling water he likes

name: Jordan — U.S. Gen Z
description: A 21-year-old in Austin, TX, who lives on sparkling water and is always looking for the next aesthetic brand. Has never heard of Clearly Canadian but would be receptive if it showed up on his FYP.
motivations: finding brands before they blow up
pain_points: doesn't know the brand
not available at his usual HEB or 7-Eleven
price vs. Liquid Death or LaCroix
buying_triggers: spotted at Whole Foods or Urban Outfitters
objections: 'Why is it in a glass bottle?'
language_style: short, Gen Z slang, hype-driven, skeptical until proven
behavior_rules: Buys something immediately if a creator he follows mentions it
Will not repurchase unless taste meets expectations on first try
Judges a beverage brand by its packaging before reading any copy
Posts an unboxing or first-taste if the packaging is interesting

name: Priya — U.S. Millennial
description: A 33-year-old in Chicago who switched from soda to sparkling water and currently buys Perrier and Bubly. Open to Canadian brands if the quality story is clear.
pain_points: Clearly Canadian not on her usual store's shelf
premium price without established brand trust
unclear where to find it
buying_triggers: sees it at Whole Foods or specialty grocer
side-by-side comparison with Perrier wins on flavor
decision_style: research-heavy, reads reviews before committing to a new brand
behavior_rules: Never adds a new beverage brand to her weekly shop without trying a single unit first
Compares two or three alternatives online before making a premium purchase
Strongly influenced by friend recommendations over advertising
Willing to pay up to 20% premium if the brand story is compelling

name: Diane — Nostalgia Loyalist
description: A 44-year-old in Hamilton who drank Clearly Canadian throughout the 90s and was delighted when the brand returned. Buys a case whenever she sees it and has introduced it to her kids.
motivations: sharing a Canadian icon with family
pain_points: notices formulation feels different than she remembers
frustrated when stock is inconsistent at her grocery
price has gone up
buying_triggers: sees it on the shelf — doesn't need a reason
her kids ask for it after trying it
objections: 'Why can't I find it at my Loblaws every time?'
channel_preferences: direct online if grocery is out
behavior_rules: Buys the last bottles on the shelf when stock is low to 'save' them
Will switch stores if her regular grocery is out of stock
Actively recommends the brand to friends unprompted
Would participate in a loyalty or subscription program if offered

name: Alex — Health-Conscious Buyer
description: A 31-year-old in Calgary who tracks macros, avoids all added sugar, and is skeptical of 'zero sugar' claims until he verifies the ingredient list himself.
pain_points: skeptical that zero sugar versions taste good
wants to see full ingredient transparency
has been burned by artificial sweetener aftertaste before
buying_triggers: 'zero sugar' label combined with natural sweetener claim
positive taste review from a fitness influencer
objections: 'Is this actually spring water or just filtered tap?'
decision_style: research-heavy, label-reader, takes time at shelf
language_style: analytical, asks specific questions, responds to evidence and data
behavior_rules: Will not buy if sugar is above 0g
Reads the full ingredient list before first purchase
Googles brand background before committing to a regular buy
Will switch brands immediately if a cleaner option appears

name: Sophie — Premium Lifestyle Buyer
description: A 38-year-old in Montreal who buys San Pellegrino for dinner parties and selects beverages the way she selects wine. The glass bottle and Canadian story could convert her.
motivations: serving something guests will ask about
pain_points: cans feel too casual for her use case
doesn't know the brand well enough to serve at a dinner party
needs to see it in a premium retail context first
buying_triggers: glass bottle format on a premium shelf
sees it served at a restaurant or hotel
a sommelier or food writer mentions it
objections: 'I'd never serve cans at a dinner'
'Is this actually premium or just packaged that way?'
decision_style: deliberate, brand-story-driven, influenced by social context
language_style: considered, taste-literate, asks about provenance, social-context driven
behavior_rules: Will not bring a beverage to a dinner party that she hasn't tasted first
Makes purchasing decisions based on how a product looks on a table
Pays premium without hesitation if the brand story is coherent
Always researches provenance of food/beverage before committing

name: Devon — Convenience-First Buyer
description: A 26-year-old in Ottawa who grabs a cold drink at the gas station or corner store every day after the gym. Brand loyalty is low — whoever is cold and available wins.
pain_points: can't find Clearly Canadian cans at his regular stops
glass bottle is impractical on the go
price above $2.50 is a friction point
buying_triggers: cold can visible in the front row of the case
objections: 'I've never seen this at my gas station'
'A glass bottle in my gym bag? No'
'If it's more than $2.50 I'm getting a Bubly'
decision_style: fully impulse, availability-driven, no prior brand intent
language_style: casual, practical, low-attention, responds to simple clear cues
behavior_rules: Never walks past the cold case without grabbing something
Will not pay over $2.50 for a single-serve sparkling water
Chooses whatever is at eye level and cold
Has no loyalty — will switch brands based on what's available today
